Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Player moves horizontally in sky-blue corridors (cols 13-53, rows 19-62). Yellow follows at +2 col offset. Two sky-blue regions: lower main corridor and upper-right area (rows 0-11). 7 green blocks to collect (3 top, 4 middle). Target ~21 actions for level.
- Goal model: Navigate to collect all 7 green blocks. Need vertical movement method.
- Action model: LEFT/RIGHT move horizontally. MOUSE might enable vertical movement or direct positioning.
- Plan: Test MOUSE on a position above to see if it moves the player vertically. Also continue RIGHT to explore horizontal range.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 1 of about 21 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see the layout more clearly:
- Column 27: Green block at rows 1-5 (top), then sky blue from row 19 to 29, then player at rows 37-40. There's a gap (black) between rows 6-18 and rows 30-36.
- Column 35: Sky blue from row 0-11 (upper region), green blocks at rows 13-17 (middle), then sky blue from row 19-29, then sky blue at rows 37-40 (lower region). There's a gap between rows 12, 18, and 30-36.
So the sky blue corridors have gaps. The player can only move within connected sky blue regions. The upper region (rows 0-11) and lower region (rows 19-29, then 37-62) are NOT directly connected vertically.
Wait, but the player is at rows 37-40 and the sky blue goes up to row 29. There's a gap at rows 30-36. How does the player get to the upper areas?
Let me reconsider. Maybe MOUSE is used to click on positions and the player teleports there. Or maybe ACTION7 does something special.
Let me try MOUSE on a green block to see what happens. If the goal is to collect green blocks, clicking on them might be the way.
Actually, looking at the layout again:
- The player is in the lower sky blue region (rows 37-62)
- There's a middle sky blue region (rows 19-29) 
- There's an upper sky blue region (rows 0-11)
- Green blocks are at rows 1-5 (top) and rows 13-17 (middle)
The green blocks seem to be placed ON TOP of or BETWEEN the sky blue regions. Maybe the goal is to click on them with MOUSE to collect them, and LEFT/RIGHT moves the player to position under the target block.
Let me try MOUSE on a green block position to see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Sky-blue corridors exist in 3 disconnected vertical bands (rows 0-11, 19-29, 37-62). Player is in the lower band. Green blocks sit in the upper (rows 1-5) and middle (rows 13-17) areas. LEFT/RIGHT moves player horizontally within the connected corridor.
Goal model: Collect 7 green blocks. MOUSE likely clicks/collects blocks when player is aligned.
Action model: LEFT/RIGHT for horizontal movement. MOUSE for vertical interaction or collection.
Plan: Try MOUSE on a green block to test collection mechanic. If that doesn't work, try ACTION7.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try MOUSE on a green block to see if it collects it
# The green blocks are at specific positions. Let me try clicking on one.
# Green block at col 32-34, row 14 (middle area)

# First, let me check if there's a vertical passage from player position to the green blocks
# The player is at col 26-27. Let me see what's directly above in the sky blue
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')

# Check the gap between lower and middle sky blue
print("Rows 30-36, cols 25-30:")
for r in range(30, 37):
    segment = ascii_lines[r][25:30]
    print(f"  row {r}: '{segment}'")

# Try MOUSE on a green block
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 14, 'col': 33}])
print(f"\nMOUSE result: {result}")

# Check if anything changed
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
greens = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'N']
print(f"Green blocks after MOUSE: {len(greens)}")
for g in greens:
    print(f"  id={g['id']} boundary={g['boundary']}")

player = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'b'][0]
print(f"Player: boundary={player['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
